explanation_md: `a == b` compares `10 == 10`, which is `true` (printed as `1`). `a != c` calls `!(*this == rhs)` which is `!(10 == 20)` = `!false` = `true` (printed as `1`). Output: `1 1`.

prompt_md: You have defined `operator<` for a `Weight` class. Which is the correct way to define `operator>`?

options.b: `bool operator>(const Weight& rhs) const { return rhs < *this; }`
options.c: `bool operator>(const Weight& rhs) const { return kg > rhs.kg; }`

explanation_md: Delegating `a > b` as `b < a` reuses the already-defined `operator<`, ensuring the two operators stay consistent if the ordering logic ever changes. Option (c) duplicates logic and can diverge. Option (d) is incorrect because `!(a < b)` means `a >= b`, not `a > b`, and it also omits `const`.
`a < b`: both have `x == 2`, so we compare `y`: `5 < 3` is `false` (0). `c < a`: `c.x = 1 < a.x = 2`, so it returns `true` immediately (1). Output: `0 1`.

prompt_md: Why should comparison operator member functions be declared `const`?

options.a: So the returned `bool` cannot be modified
options.b: So the operators can be called on `const`-qualified objects and don't accidentally modify either operand
options.c: Because the C++ standard requires `const` on all comparison operators
options.d: So that the compiler can optimize the comparison at compile time

explanation_md: Marking the member function `const` promises that it does not modify `*this`, which is the correct semantic for a comparison (it only inspects the object). It also makes the operator callable on `const` references. The other options are incorrect: `const` on the function does not affect the return value, the standard does not mandate it, and `const` membership has nothing to do with compile-time optimization.

options.a: The `(double)` cast should be `(float)` for correct precision
options.b: Floating-point division of `1/3` and `2/6` may not compare equal due to precision loss; cross-multiply instead
options.c: `operator==` must be a free function, not a member
options.d: The `den` member should be initialized to `0`, not `1`

explanation_md: Converting to `double` before dividing can produce slightly different bit patterns for mathematically equal fractions. The correct approach is integer cross-multiplication: `num * rhs.den == rhs.num * den`. Casting to `float` makes precision worse, not better. `operator==` can be a member, and the default initializer of `den` to `1` is correct.
